1: הוֹדוּ לַיהוָה כִּי-טוֹב כִּי לְעוֹלָם חַסְדּוֹ (hôdû layhwāh kî-ṭôb kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô, "Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever!") — the hallel formula (Psalms 100:5; Psalms 106:1; Psalms 118:1,29; 136 — where it forms the refrain after every verse). Hôdû: acknowledge, give thanks (from yādāh: to extend the hand in acknowledgment — both to confess and to praise). Kî-ṭôb: because he is good (not merely because of his benefits). Kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô: because his steadfast love endures forever — the unbounded duration of the covenant commitment that makes giving thanks not merely appropriate but safe: the God thanked will not withdraw. Psalm 107 is the only psalm to open with the hallel formula and then follow it with four extended narrative strophes.
2: יֹאמְרוּ גְּאוּלֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר גְּאָלָם מִיַּד-צָר (yōʾmĕrû gĕʾûlê yĕhwāh ʾăsher gĕʾālām miyyad-ṣār, "Let those the LORD has redeemed declare it — those he bought back from the grip of the enemy!") — gĕʾûlê yĕhwāh: the redeemed of the LORD (from gāʾal: the kinsman-redeemer word — the family obligation to buy back a relative from slavery or debt — Leviticus 25:25,47-55; Ruth 3-4; Isaiah 41:14; Psalms 43:1,14). This is not a commercial purchase but a covenantal rescue by the divine next-of-kin. Miyyad-ṣār: from the hand of the adversary/oppressor (the grip of the enemy treated as a physical clench from which the redeemed must be pried). The psalm summons this specific community — the redeemed — as its primary narrators.
3: וּמֵאֲרָצוֹת קִבְּצָם מִמִּזְרָח וּמִמַּעֲרָב מִצָּפוֹן וּמִיָּם (ûmêʾărāṣôt qibbĕṣām mimmizrāḥ ûmimmaʿărāb miṣṣāpôn ûmiyyām, "gathered in from every land — from east and west, from north and south!") — the four compass directions (east, west, north, and yām = the sea/south in Israel's orientation where the west = the Mediterranean and "south" was often specified as sea). The exile-return motif: God gathered scattered Israel from the whole world (Isaiah 43:5-6: "I will bring your offspring from the east and gather you from the west; I will say to the north, 'Give them up'"; Deuteronomy 30:3-5). The geographical comprehensiveness grounds the prayer for any exile from any direction.
4: תָּעוּ בַמִּדְבָּר בִּישִׁימוֹן דָּרֶךְ עִיר מוֹשָׁב לֹא מָצָאוּ (tāʿû bammidbar bîshîmôn dārek ʿîr môshāb lōʾ māṣāʾû, "Some wandered in the desert waste, finding no road to a city where they could live!") — tāʿû: they wandered (the word for going astray — sheep without a shepherd: Isaiah 53:6: "we all like sheep have gone astray"). Bammidbar bîshîmôn: in the wilderness, in the desert/waste (the doubled synonyms emphasizing the uninhabited, trackless character of the place). Tōhû in Hebrew: desert-waste (the word of Genesis 1:2 for the pre-creation formlessness — the wanderers are in a pre-civilizational void where there is no city, no road, no môshāb/habitation). The Exodus wilderness is the paradigm.
5: רְעֵבִים גַּם-צְמֵאִים נַפְשָׁם בָּהֶם תִּתְעַטָּף (rĕʿêbîm gam-ṣĕmêʾîm napshām bāhem titʿaṭṭāp, "Hungry and thirsty, their soul grew faint within them!") — rĕʿêbîm gam-ṣĕmêʾîm: hungry and also thirsty — the two fundamental physical needs in the wilderness. Napshām bāhem titʿaṭṭāp: "their soul fainted within them" (ʿāṭap: to be weak, to faint — Psalms 61:2; Psalms 77:3; Lamentations 2:11,19: souls fainting in the city streets). The nefesh (soul/life-breath) is reduced to fainting — the vital force nearly extinguished. This physical extremity is the trigger for the cry of v6.
6: וַיִּצְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם מִמְּצוּקוֹתֵיהֶם יַצִּילֵם (wayyiṣʿăqû ʾel-yĕhwāh baṣṣar lāhem mimmĕṣûqôtêhem yaṣṣîlêm, "Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he rescued them from their distresses!") — the first occurrence of the cry-response pattern that repeats in all four strophes (vv6/13/19/28). Ṣāʿaq: to cry out (the urgent, loud cry of distress — Exodus 2:23: "the Israelites groaned and cried out"; Psalms 8:12: Moses cried out). Baṣṣar lāhem: in their tight/narrow place (the distress of being squeezed — Psalms 18:6; Psalms 102:2). Mimmĕṣûqôtêhem yaṣṣîlêm: from their distresses he rescued them — immediate, specific, effective rescue.
7: וַיַּדְרִיכֵם בְּדֶרֶךְ יְשָׁרָה לָלֶכֶת אֶל-עִיר מוֹשָׁב (wayyadrîkhêm bĕderek yĕshārāh lāleket ʾel-ʿîr môshāb, "He guided them on a straight path to a city where they could settle!") — hidrîk (hiphil of dārak: to tread a path — to walk, but here causatively: to cause to walk, to guide). The one who guides the blind on a road they did not know (Isaiah 42:16). Bĕderek yĕshārāh: on a straight/level path (the opposite of the trackless waste of v4). ʿÎr môshāb: a city of habitation — the specific answer to the specific need of v4 (finding "no road to a city"). The divine guidance creates the very road and destination that was utterly absent.
8: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם (yôdû layhwāh ḥasdô wĕnipĕlĕʾōtāyw libnê ʾādām, "Let them give thanks to the LORD for his steadfast love and for his wonders done for the children of man!") — the first refrain (repeated verbatim at vv15, 21, 31 — the structural spine of the psalm). Yôdû: let them give thanks (the same root as hôdû in v1). Ḥasdô: his steadfast love (the covenant-faithfulness). Nipĕlĕʾōtāyw libnê ʾādām: his wonders for the sons of Adam/humanity (the acts that exceed human comprehension — Exodus 15:11; Psalms 77:11). The refrain connects each rescue to the same two divine attributes: the character (ḥesed) and the actions (nipĕlāʾôt).
9: כִּי-הִשְׂבִּיעַ נֶפֶשׁ שֹׁקֵקָה וְנֶפֶשׁ רְעֵבָה מִלֵּא-טוֹב (kî-hisbîaʿ nefesh shōqēqāh wĕnefesh rĕʿêbāh millêʾ-ṭôb, "For he gives what the hungry soul longs for and fills the starving soul with good things!") — the explanatory kî-clause expanding the refrain with the specific act. Nefesh shōqēqāh: the longing/thirsting soul (shāqaq: to rush, to long — the intense craving of the dehydrated). Hisbîaʿ (hiphil: to satisfy to the full — the same verb as Psalms 17:15: "I shall be satisfied when I awake"; Psalms 22:26: the afflicted shall eat and be satisfied). Millêʾ-ṭôb: filled with good things — Luke 1:53 echoes this: "he has filled the hungry with good things and the rich he has sent away empty."
10: יֹשְׁבֵי חֹשֶׁךְ וְצַלְמָוֶת אֲסִירֵי עֳנִי וּבַרְזֶל (yōshĕbê ḥōshek wĕṣalmāwet ʾăsîrê ʿŏnî ûbarzel, "Some sat in darkness and the shadow of death, bound in misery and iron chains!") — the second strophe (vv10-16): the prisoners. Ṣalmāwet: shadow of death (the Hebrew compound of ṣēl/shadow and māwet/death — the darkest darkness imaginable, the gloom of the underworld entrance — Job 3:5; Psalms 10:21-22; Psalms 24:17; Psalms 23:4). This word appears in Isaiah 9:1 ("people who walked in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of the shadow of death, light has dawned") — quoted in Matthew 4:16 as Jesus's coming to Galilee. ʾĂsîrê ʿŏnî ûbarzel: bound in affliction and iron — the double constraint of suffering and physical chains.
11: כִּי-מָרוּ אִמְרֵי אֵל וַעֲצַת עֶלְיוֹן נָאָצוּ (kî-mārû ʾimrê ʾêl waʿăṣat ʿelyôn nāʾāṣû, "for they had defied the commands of God and rejected the plan of the Most High!") — the explanation of the imprisonment: willful rebellion against the divine word. Mārāh: to be contentious, to rebel (Numbers 20:24; Deuteronomy 1:26,43: Israel's rebellion in the wilderness). ʾImrê ʾêl: the words/commands of God. ʿĂṣat ʿelyôn nāʾāṣû: they spurned/despised the counsel of the Most High (nāʾaṣ: to treat with contempt — Numbers 14:11: "how long will they treat me with contempt?"). The connection between sin and bondage is explicit: the darkness is the consequence of defiance. But the refrain's rescue (v14) happens despite the cause.
12: וַיַּכְנַע בֶּעָמָל לִבָּם כָּשְׁלוּ וְאֵין עֹזֵר (wayyakhnаʿ beʿāmāl libbām kāshĕlû wĕʾên ʿōzêr, "So he wore them down with grinding toil; they stumbled and fell, with no one to help!") — kānaʿ (hiphil: to humble, to subjugate — the divine bringing-low as discipline): God employed their own consequence to break their resistance. Beʿāmāl: in/through toil/misery (the labor that is also suffering — Ecclesiastes 1:3; Psalms 2:10). Kāshĕlû wĕʾên ʿōzêr: they stumbled and there was no helper — the consequence of the defiance of v11 is isolation: the one who rejected divine counsel finds no human counsel available either.
13: וַיִּזְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם מִמְּצוּקוֹתֵיהֶם יוֹשִׁיעֵם (wayyizʿăqû ʾel-yĕhwāh baṣṣar lāhem mimmĕṣûqôtêhem yôshîʿêm, "Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he saved them from their distresses!") — the second occurrence of the cry-and-rescue pattern. Note the variation: v6 uses yaṣṣîlêm (he rescued/snatched away) while v13 uses yôshîʿêm (he saved — yāshaʿ: the verb behind yĕshûʿāh/salvation and the name Jesus). The two verbs are synonymous in rescue-language but yāshaʿ carries the additional freight of the Exodus salvation (Exodus 14:30: "the LORD saved Israel that day") and the messianic name.
14: יוֹצִיאֵם מֵחֹשֶׁךְ וְצַלְמָוֶת וּמוֹסְרוֹתֵיהֶם יְנַתֵּק (yôṣîʾêm mêḥōshek wĕṣalmāwet ûmôsĕrôtêhem yĕnattêq, "He led them out of the darkness and the shadow of death and broke their bonds apart!") — yôṣîʾ (hiphil: to bring out — the Exodus verb: Exodus 13:3,9,14,16: "the LORD brought you out"). From ḥōshek wĕṣalmāwet (the darkest dark of v10) into freedom. Yĕnattêq: he snapped/broke apart (nātaq: to tear loose, to sever — Acts 2:24 quotes Psalms 16:10 with the same semantic field for the resurrection). The iron chains of v10 are broken by the one who does wonders.
15: The second refrain, identical to v8: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם (yôdû layhwāh ḥasdô wĕnipĕlĕʾōtāyw libnê ʾādām) — "Let them give thanks to the LORD for his steadfast love and for his wonders done for the children of man!" The refrain's exact repetition after each rescue is the psalm's liturgical genius: the same God who rescued the wanderers from the desert (v4) rescued the prisoners from the darkness. The identical words applied to different situations assert a single unified character behind all the rescues.
16: כִּי-שִׁבַּר דַּלְתוֹת נְחֹשֶׁת וּבְרִיחֵי בַרְזֶל גִּדֵּעַ (kî-shibbir daltôt nĕḥōshet ûbĕrîḥê barzel giddêaʿ, "For he has smashed the gates of bronze and snapped the iron bars!") — the kî-clause explaining how the rescue happened. Daltôt nĕḥōshet: the bronze/copper gates (the gates of the ancient dungeon or enemy city — Psalms 9:13: "you who lift me from the gates of death"). Isaiah 45:2 uses this image for Cyrus: "I will break down the gates of bronze and cut through the bars of iron" (the Persian king as the LORD's instrument for releasing captives). The messianic application: the gates of death shattered (Matthew 16:18: "the gates of Hades will not prevail").
17: אֱוִלִים מִדֶּרֶךְ פִּשְׁעָם וּמֵעֲוֹנֹתֵיהֶם יִתְעַנּוּ (ʾĕwîlîm midderek pishʿām ûmêʿăwōnōtêhem yitʿannû, "Some were fools who made themselves sick by the path they chose, suffering because of their own sins!") — the third strophe (vv17-22): the sick. ʾĔwîlîm: fools (from ʾĕwil: the dense/thick fool who lacks moral sense — Proverbs 1:7; Psalms 10:8; distinct from kĕsîl/fool-by-choice). Midderek pishʿām: because of the path of their transgressions (peshaʿ: the covenant-breaking transgression, the deliberate rebellion — Isaiah 53:5: "he was wounded for our transgressions"). The sick suffer because of their own choices: the wisdom tradition connecting moral and physical health (though vv1-3's geographical gathering suggests this strophe has a broader application than individual punishment).
18: כָּל-אֹכֶל תְּתַעֵב נַפְשָׁם וַיַּגִּיעוּ עַד-שַׁעֲרֵי מָוֶת (kol-ʾōkhel tĕtaʿêb napshām wayyaggîʿû ʿad-shaʿărê māwet, "Their soul could not bear any food, and they were approaching the very gates of death!") — tĕtaʿêb: it loathes, abominates (the absolute revulsion of the severely ill who cannot eat — the same word for the loathing of what is detestable in Leviticus 11:11,13). Shaʿărê māwet: the gates of death (death conceived as a city or underworld with gates — Psalms 9:13; Job 38:17: "have the gates of death been shown to you?"; Isaiah 38:10: Hezekiah's lament). The gates not yet entered but imminent — the most extreme medical crisis the ancient world knew.
19: The third cry-and-rescue: וַיִּזְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם מִמְּצוּקוֹתֵיהֶם יוֹשִׁיעֵם — identical to v13 (same strophe-formula). The repetition of the identical cry in the third strophe with a different affliction (sickness vs imprisonment) reinforces the psalm's universalism: the same cry works across all categories of suffering. The one who sent them into the prison of their folly (v17) is also the one who receives their cry and rescues.
20: יִשְׁלַח דְּבָרוֹ וְיִרְפָּאֵם וִימַלֵּט מִשַּׁחִיתוֹתָם (yishlah dĕbārô wĕyirpāʾêm wîmallêṭ mishshaḥîtôtām, "He sent his word and healed them and pulled them back from the pit of destruction!") — yishlah dĕbārô: he sent his word (the active, personified word dispatched as a divine agent — Psalms 33:6: "by the word of the LORD the heavens were made"; Isaiah 55:11: "my word shall not return to me empty"). The Word sent to heal anticipates John 1:1,14 and the healing ministry of the Word made flesh (Matthew 8:16: "he cast out the spirits with a word and healed all who were sick"). Shaḥîtôtām: their pits/destructions (the plural of shaḥat: pit, corruption — used for both the physical grave and moral destruction — Psalms 16:10: "you will not let your Holy One see corruption"; the resurrection connection).
21: The third refrain: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם — repeated verbatim. The fourfold structural use of this refrain is itself a theological act: the same praise-formula covers the rescue of wanderers, prisoners, sick, and sailors. No rescue is too small or too specific for the grand ḥesed; no sufferer falls outside the category of "children of man" for whom the wonders are done.
22: וְיִזְבְּחוּ זִבְחֵי תוֹדָה וִיסַפְּרוּ מַעֲשָׂיו בְּרִנָּה (wĕyizbĕḥû zibĕḥê tôdāh wîsappĕrû maʿăśāyw bĕrinnāh, "Let them offer thanksgiving sacrifices and tell of his deeds with songs of joy!") — zibĕḥê tôdāh: thanksgiving sacrifices (Leviticus 7:12-15 — the specific offering of the tôdāh feast, eaten the same day, shared with family and the poor, accompanied by leavened and unleavened bread). The recovery from sickness calls for the most communal and joyful sacrifice. Bĕrinnāh: with ringing joy (the shout of glad exuberance — Psalms 100:2). The rescue is not merely private: it becomes public proclamation (sappĕrû maʿăśāyw: narrate/recount his deeds — the word for the storytelling that preserves the memory of divine acts).
23: יוֹרְדֵי הַיָּם בָּאֳנִיּוֹת עֹשֵׂי מְלָאכָה בְּמַיִם רַבִּים (yôrĕdê hayyām bāʾŏniyyôt ʿōśê mĕlākhāh bĕmayim rabbîm, "Some went down to the sea in ships, plying their trade on the wide and open sea!") — the fourth strophe (vv23-32): the sailors. Yōrĕdê hayyām: those who go down to the sea (the idiom for seafaring — "going down" because ships were launched from harbor to the lower sea-surface). ʿŌśê mĕlākhāh: doing their work/trade (the word mĕlākhāh covers both skilled craftsmanship and occupational work — used for the work of the tabernacle builders). Bĕmayim rabbîm: in the great waters (the sea as the mayim rabbîm of Psalms 29:3; Psalms 93:4 — the cosmic chaos-waters that only YHWH controls).
24: הֵמָּה רָאוּ מַעֲשֵׂי יְהוָה וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו בִּמְצוּלָה (hêmmāh rāʾû maʿăśê yĕhwāh wĕnipĕlĕʾōtāyw bimĕṣûlāh, "They witnessed what the LORD does — his wonders in the depths!") — maʿăśê yĕhwāh: the works of the LORD (the acts of creation and sustaining — Psalms 8:3; Psalms 19:1; Psalms 104:24). Bimĕṣûlāh: in the deep (the abyss of the ocean — Exodus 15:5: "they sank into the depths like a stone"; Psalms 69:2,15: the deep waters). The sailors are involuntary witnesses to the divine power in the most fearsome natural environment.
25: וַיֹּאמֶר וַיַּעֲמֵד רוּחַ סְעָרָה וַתְּרוֹמֵם גַּלָּיו (wayyōʾmer wayyaʿămêd rûaḥ sĕʿārāh wattĕrômêm gallāyw, "He spoke and a storm-wind rose, whipping the sea into waves!") — wayyōʾmer: "and he said/spoke" — the bare divine speech as the cause of the storm (as in Gen 1: God speaks and things happen). Rûaḥ sĕʿārāh: a storm-wind (the same compound in Job 38:1; Ezekiel 1:4 — the divine storm from which God speaks). Wattĕrômêm gallāyw: and it lifted up its waves (the storm raising the sea into towering waves — Job 38:8-11: "who shut in the sea with doors?"). The storm is directly commanded by God and directly stilled by him in v29 — the episode of Jonah 1 and the disciples' storm (Matthew 8:23-27; Mark 4:35-41) are explicitly designed as fulfillments of this pattern.
26: יַעֲלוּ שָׁמַיִם יֵרְדוּ תְהוֹמוֹת נַפְשָׁם בְּרָעָה תִתְמוֹגָג (yaʿălû shāmayim yêrĕdû tĕhômôt napshām bĕrāʿāh titmmôgāg, "They heaved up to the sky and plunged back down to the depths; their soul was melting away in terror!") — the graphic description of being tossed in a storm from the sailors' perspective: sky one moment, abyss the next. Tĕhômôt: the deeps/tehom (Genesis 1:2 — the primordial deep; Psalms 77:16; Psalms 104:6). Napshām bĕrāʿāh titmmôgāg: "their soul melted away in their trouble" (māgag: to melt, to dissolve — Joshua 2:11: Rahab says "our hearts melted" at Israel's approach). The physical motion (sky/depths) produces psychological dissolution.
27: יָחוֹגּוּ וְיָנוּעוּ כַּשִּׁכּוֹר וְכָל-חָכְמָתָם תִּתְבַּלָּע (yāḥôggû wĕyānûʿû kashshikkôr wĕkhol-ḥokhmātām titbāllāʿ, "They lurched and stumbled like drunken men, and all their skill/wisdom was consumed!") — yāḥôggû: to reel, to stagger (from ḥāgag: the circular festival dance; here the uncontrolled staggering of a drunk or seasick man). ḥokhmātām titbāllāʿ: "all their wisdom was swallowed up" (bālaʿ: to swallow — Numbers 16:30-32: the earth opened and swallowed Korah's company; Exodus 7:12: Aaron's staff swallowed the magicians' staffs). The sailors' skill and wisdom — the very things that qualified them to work "in great waters" (v23) — are swallowed by the chaos. Human competence exhausted; the cry (v28) becomes the only remaining option.
28: The fourth cry: וַיִּצְעֲקוּ אֶל-יְהוָה בַּצַּר לָהֶם וּמִמְּצוּקֹתֵיהֶם יוֹצִיאֵם (wayyiṣʿăqû ʾel-yĕhwāh baṣṣar lāhem ûmimmĕṣûqōtêhem yôṣîʾêm) — the fourth occurrence of the cry-rescue pattern, now using yôṣîʾ (he brought out — the Exodus verb of vv13-14). The cry comes after all human competence is exhausted (v27: wisdom swallowed). The pattern is deliberately structured: rescue does not come until all self-reliance is eliminated.
29: יָקֵם סְעָרָה לִדְמָמָה וַיֶּחֱשׁוּ גַּלֵּיהֶם (yāqêm sĕʿārāh lîdĕmāmāh wayyeḥĕshû gallêhem, "He calmed the storm to a stillness, and the waves fell silent!") — yāqêm sĕʿārāh lîdĕmāmāh: "he raised the storm to a stillness" — the unusual idiom: the storm is "raised/established" (qûm) into (lĕ) stillness (dĕmāmāh). The same God who said "he spoke and a storm-wind rose" (v25) now speaks the storm into silence. Dĕmāmāh: the stillness, hush (1 Kings 19:12: the "still small voice" — literally "voice of fine/thin silence" — qôl dĕmāmāh daqqāh — after the storm). Mark 4:39: "he rebuked the wind and said to the sea, 'Peace! Be still!' And the wind ceased and there was a great calm."
30: וַיִּשְׂמְחוּ כִי-יִשְׁתֹּקוּ וַיַּנְחֵם אֶל-מְחוֹז חֶפְצָם (wayyiśmĕḥû kî-yishtōqû wayyanhêm ʾel-mĕḥôz ḥepṣām, "They rejoiced as the sea grew calm, and he guided them into the harbor they had longed for!") — mĕḥôz ḥepṣām: the harbor of their longing/desire (a hapax — mĕḥôz appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible; ḥêpeṣ: delight, desire — the harbor they most wanted to reach). The rescue is not merely survival but the attainment of the original goal: the sailors set out to reach a destination (v23: working their trade) and the rescue brings them exactly there. Grace restores the original design.
31: The fourth and final refrain: יוֹדוּ לַיהוָה חַסְדּוֹ וְנִפְלְאוֹתָיו לִבְנֵי אָדָם — the last of the fourfold liturgical punctuation. Having covered desert wanderers, prisoners, sick, and sailors — the full human range of suffering — the psalm now turns in vv32-43 to a wisdom epilogue that interprets the fourfold pattern cosmically.
32: וִירוֹמְמוּהוּ בִּקְהַל-עָם וּבְמוֹשַׁב זְקֵנִים יְהַלְלוּהוּ (wîrômĕmûhû biqhal-ʿām ûbĕmôshab zĕqênîm yĕhallĕlûhû, "Let them lift up his name in the great assembly and honor him in the council of the elders!") — the public dimension of rescue: the worship of the individual rescued is to be proclaimed biqhal-ʿām (in the congregation of the people — the full covenant assembly). Môshab zĕqênîm: the session of the elders (the judicial and leadership council — the equivalent of the city-gate assembly). Rescue generates testimony; testimony generates corporate praise. The fourfold rescue is not complete until it reaches the congregational gathering.
33: יָשֵׂם נְהָרוֹת לְמִדְבָּר וּמֹצָאֵי מַיִם לְצִמָּאוֹן (yāśêm nĕhārôt lĕmidbar ûmōṣāʾê mayim lĕṣimmāʾôn, "He turns rivers into desert and springs of water into parched ground!") — the wisdom epilogue begins (vv33-43): the cosmic pattern of divine reversal. God can turn fertility into barrenness. Nĕhārôt lĕmidbar: rivers into wilderness (Isaiah 50:2: "I rebuke the sea and make it dry"; Psalms 66:6). The productive river system (civilization's lifeblood) made into trackless desert. The pattern establishes divine sovereignty over both agricultural fertility and barrenness.
34: אֶרֶץ פְּרִי לִמְלֵחָה מֵרָעַת יֹשְׁבֵי בָהּ (ʾereṣ pĕrî limlêḥāh mêrāʿat yōshĕbê bāh, "fertile land into a barren salt flat — for the wickedness of those who live there!") — ʾereṣ pĕrî limlêḥāh: the land of fruit/produce into salt/salt flat. Mĕlêḥāh: saltland (Genesis 14:3: the Salt Sea; Deuteronomy 29:23: the divine curse makes the land "like Sodom and Gomorrah... sulphur and salt, burned-out"; Zephaniah 2:9: Moab like Sodom). The connection to Sodom is explicit: moral wickedness produces geographic desolation. This is the positive analogue of the cursed ground (Genesis 3:17-19).
35: יָשֵׂם מִדְבָּר לַאֲגַם-מַיִם וְאֶרֶץ צִיָּה לְמֹצָאֵי מָיִם (yāśêm midbar laʾăgam-mayim wĕʾereṣ ṣiyyāh lĕmōṣāʾê māyim, "But he can also turn the desert into pools of water and dry ground into flowing springs!") — the reversal of vv33-34: what God can make barren, he can also make fertile. Midbar laʾăgam-mayim: the wilderness into a pool/lake (Isaiah 41:18: "I will make the wilderness a pool of water and the dry land springs of water"). The same sovereign power that judges wickedness with barrenness also rescues the hungry with fertility (Isaiah 35:1-7: the messianic transformation of the desert into a garden).
36: וַיּוֹשֶׁב שָׁם רְעֵבִים וַיְכוֹנְנוּ עִיר מוֹשָׁב (wayyôsheb shām rĕʿêbîm waykônĕnû ʿîr môshāb, "He brings the hungry to live there, and they build a city to call home!") — the same vocabulary as v4: the wanderers who found "no road to a city where they could live" (ʿîr môshāb) now build one in the transformed wilderness. The first strophe's rescue (vv4-9) is re-enacted at the cosmic/agricultural level: God turns the desert that the wanderers could not cross into the very city they could not find. The divine reversal is not abstract but creates human habitation.
37: וַיִּזְרְעוּ שָׂדוֹת וַיִּטְּעוּ כְרָמִים וַיַּעֲשׂוּ פְּרִי תְבוּאָה (wayyizrĕʿû śādôt wayyiṭṭĕʿû kĕrāmîm wayyaʿăśû pĕrî tĕbûʾāh, "They plant fields and vineyards that bring in an abundant crop!") — the agricultural acts that civilization requires: planting fields and vineyards (the grain and fruit that together represent food and drink — Deuteronomy 8:8). Pĕrî tĕbûʾāh: fruit of produce/harvest (the abundant yield that exceeds subsistence). The newly settled community creates the agricultural civilization that was impossible in the desert of v4. Creation's mandate (Genesis 1:28: "be fruitful and multiply") fulfilled in the transformed wilderness.
38: וַיְבָרְכֵם וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד וּבְהֶמְתָּם לֹא יַמְעִיט (waybārĕkhêm wayyirbû mĕʾōd ûbĕhemtām lōʾ yamʿîṭ, "He blesses them and they grow in number, and he keeps their herds from shrinking!") — waybārĕkhêm wayyirbû mĕʾōd: "he blessed them and they multiplied greatly" — the creation blessing of Genesis 1:22,28 ("be fruitful and multiply") enacted in historical time. Ûbĕhemtām lōʾ yamʿîṭ: "and their cattle he does not diminish" — the livestock as the measure of agricultural blessing (Genesis 13:2; Deuteronomy 7:13-14). Divine blessing expressed in demographic and pastoral abundance.
39: וַיִּמְעֲטוּ וַיָּשֹׁחוּ מֵעֹצֶר רָעָה וְיָגוֹן (wayyimʿăṭû wayyāshōḥû mêʿōṣer rāʿāh wĕyāgôn, "But when they are cut down and humbled through oppression, calamity, and grief!") — the reversal returns: the same community that multiplied (v38) can be reduced (mĕʿaṭ: to be few/small — Leviticus 26:22: "your animals will be few"). ʿŌṣer: oppression, constraint; rāʿāh: calamity/evil; yāgôn: grief/sorrow. The three negative forces that collapse the abundance of vv36-38. The historical pattern: the blessing of settlement followed by oppression (Judges cycle).
40: שֹׁפֵךְ בּוּז עַל-נְדִיבִים וַיַּתְעֵם בְּתֹהוּ לֹא-דָרֶךְ (shōpêkh bûz ʿal-nĕdîbîm wayyatʿêm bĕtōhû lōʾ-dārek, "he pours contempt on rulers and sends them wandering in pathless deserts!") — bûz: contempt, dishonor (the opposite of kābôd/honor — Proverbs 12:8; the divine valuation applied inversely). ʿAl-nĕdîbîm: on princes/nobles/generous ones — those who had status and power. Wayyatʿêm bĕtōhû lōʾ-dārek: he makes them wander in pathless wasteland (tōhû: the same formlessness-waste as Genesis 1:2 and v4). The leaders who built the city (v36) are returned to the desert by the same divine sovereignty that created the city. Job 12:24 quotes this verse almost verbatim.
41: וַיְשַׂגֵּב אֶבְיוֹן מֵעוֹנִי וַיָּשֶׂם כַּצֹּאן מִשְׁפָּחוֹת (wayyĕśaggêb ʾebyôn mêʿônî wayyāśem kaṣṣōʾn mishpāḥôt, "but he raises the poor person up from their misery and makes their family grow like a flock!") — the simultaneous reversal: while the nobles wander in the wasteland (v40), the poor person is elevated. ʾEbyôn: the desperately poor/needy. Śāgab (piel: to set on high, to lift to safety — Psalms 59:1; Psalms 91:14; Proverbs 29:25: "whoever trusts in the LORD is kept safe"). Kaṣṣōʾn mishpāḥôt: like a flock, families (from the shepherd-flock image of v3's gathering). The divine reversal runs simultaneously: nobles down, poor up. Hannah's song (1 Samuel 2:4-8) and the Magnificat (Luke 1:51-53) are the clearest developments of this pattern.
42: יִרְאוּ יְשָׁרִים וְיִשְׂמָחוּ וְכָל-עַוְלָה קָפְצָה פִּיהָ (yirʾû yĕshārîm wĕyiśmāḥû wĕkhol-ʿawlāh qāpĕṣāh pîhāh, "The righteous see all this and rejoice, while every mouth of wickedness is stopped!") — yĕshārîm: the upright/straight ones (those whose inner orientation is aligned with the divine character — Psalms 33:1; Psalms 97:11). Their joy is epistemological: they see and understand what is happening in the divine reversal. ʿAwlāh qāpĕṣāh pîhāh: "wickedness stops its mouth" (qāpaṣ: to close suddenly, to snap shut — the mouth of the wicked has no more to say when the divine vindication is plain). Romans 3:19: "every mouth may be stopped and the whole world held accountable to God."
43: מִי-חָכָם וְיִשְׁמָר-אֵלֶּה וְיִתְבּוֹנְנוּ חַסְדֵי יְהוָה (mî-ḥākhām wĕyishmār-ʾêlleh wĕyitbônĕnû ḥasdê yĕhwāh, "Let those who are wise take note of all this and reflect carefully on the steadfast love of the LORD!") — the wisdom epilogue's climactic verse. Mî-ḥākhām: "who is wise?" — the rhetorical summons to wisdom (Hosea 14:9: "who is wise? Let them understand these things" — the virtually identical verse at the end of Hosea; Ecclesiastes 8:1: "who is like the wise man?"). Wĕyishmār-ʾêlleh: and will keep/observe these things (the word shāmar covers both watching carefully and keeping as commandment). Wĕyitbônĕnû ḥasdê yĕhwāh: and ponder/understand the steadfast loves of the LORD (ḥasdê: the plural of ḥesed — the many expressions of the one covenant character). The whole psalm was a demonstration of the divine ḥasdê in action; wisdom is the capacity to see and understand what the four strophes have displayed.
